title: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 3
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27-40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.

The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

A student sits at a kitchen table with a page of historical dates in front of her. She covers the list, tries to recite it, fails at the third item, and begins again. Across from her, a parent says what many people have been told since childhood: "You just need to remember more." The advice sounds harmless, even obvious. Schools reward recall, workplaces value stored expertise, and digital devices promise to preserve almost everything. Yet the human mind has never worked like a cupboard into which facts can simply be packed. Remembering is only one part of mental life. Forgetting, often treated as its enemy, may be just as important.

It is easy to see why forgetting has a poor reputation. It can be inconvenient, embarrassing, and sometimes serious. A forgotten appointment wastes time; a forgotten name can damage a social exchange; a forgotten safety instruction can have consequences. But these examples can lead to a misleading conclusion: that the best possible mind would be one that kept everything. A mind unable to let go of irrelevant material would soon become crowded with details that no longer help it act. The point is not that forgetting is always good, but that memory without selection would be almost unusable.

Psychologists who study memory increasingly distinguish ordinary forgetting from carelessness or mental decline. Dr Lena Roth, a cognitive psychologist at Merrow Institute, describes useful forgetting as a kind of "cognitive housekeeping". By this she does not mean that the mind throws away information at random. Rather, it weakens access to material that is outdated, distracting, or no longer connected to present goals. This process helps explain why people often remember the general meaning of an experience while losing many of its minor details. The mind is not merely failing; it is sorting.

A study led by Pavel Innes at Carden Research Centre tested this idea using a digital navigation task. Seventy-two volunteers first learned the layout of a virtual museum, including the locations of several rooms and staircases. Half an hour later, they were told that the museum had been renovated: two staircases had been closed, and three room entrances had moved. One group was simply asked to learn the new map. A second group was first asked to describe the old routes that would no longer work and then deliberately avoid using them. In the final navigation task, this second group was slower at the beginning, but by the end they made fewer wrong turns and adapted better to the changed layout. Innes did not claim that forgetting makes people more intelligent. His conclusion was more cautious: when circumstances change, deliberately weakening old responses may make new learning more flexible.

Something similar happens in classrooms. Learning is often described as the addition of new facts, but teachers know that progress may also require students to loosen their grip on earlier misconceptions. A physics teacher, for instance, may ask students to predict which of two falling objects will land first. Many confidently choose the heavier object. After the experiment, the teacher could simply state the correct principle. Some teachers, however, first ask students to mark the contradictions in their original explanation. The aim is not to shame them for being wrong, but to make the old idea available for revision. In such cases, forgetting is not a sign of weak memory. It is part of replacing an attractive but inaccurate explanation with a better one.

Workplaces provide another example. Dario Mendez, who studies industrial training, found that experienced technicians sometimes struggle more than beginners when a familiar machine is replaced. Their difficulty is not lack of knowledge but the persistence of knowledge that once worked well. In one factory he observed, workers continued to listen for a warning sound that the new equipment no longer produced. Trainers eventually began their sessions not by introducing the new system, but by listing old habits that employees would need to abandon. This did not mean that previous expertise had no value. It meant that expertise had to be edited.

Digital technology complicates the issue further. Search engines, automatic reminders, and cloud storage have reduced the need to remember many details. Some critics argue that this weakens the mind. But the matter is not so simple. Offloading information can free attention for other tasks, just as writing once freed people from relying only on oral memory. The real question is not whether people should remember everything themselves, but whether they can judge what must be kept, what can be stored elsewhere, and what should be allowed to fade.

None of this should romanticise memory loss. Forgetting can be painful, especially when it affects identity, relationships, or health. But ordinary forgetting is not merely a defect in the machinery of thought. It can be a condition for change. To learn a better route, accept a better explanation, or adapt to a new tool, people may need not only to acquire knowledge, but also to release knowledge that has stopped serving them.

Questions 27-31
Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in boxes 27-31 on your answer sheet.

27 The writer begins with the student at the kitchen table in order to show that
A students usually study in ineffective ways.
B people commonly assume learning depends mainly on remembering more.
C parents often misunderstand the subjects their children study.
D historical knowledge is harder to remember than other kinds of knowledge.

28 What point does the writer make in the second paragraph?
A Forgetting is usually more harmful than people realise.
B The best memories are those that preserve small details.
C Remembering everything would not necessarily be useful.
D People forget things mainly because they are careless.

29 What does Dr Lena Roth's phrase "cognitive housekeeping" suggest?
A The mind may reduce access to information that is no longer useful.
B The mind stores memories in a fixed and orderly sequence.
C Forgetting happens mostly when people are physically tired.
D Forgetting is an early sign of serious mental decline.

30 What did the Carden Research Centre study suggest?
A People who forget old information always learn faster.
B New learning may improve when outdated responses are deliberately weakened.
C Digital navigation tasks are easier than real-world navigation tasks.
D Volunteers perform better when they are not told about changes.

31 What is the writer's main point about experienced technicians?
A They usually resist training because they dislike new technology.
B Their previous knowledge may sometimes interfere with adaptation.
C They are less useful in factories than beginners.
D Their errors are mainly caused by poor safety instructions.

Questions 32-36
Complete the summary using the list of words and phrases, A-I, below.
Write the correct letter, A-I, in boxes 32-36 on your answer sheet.

Forgetting in the classroom

The writer says that learning should not be seen only as the 32 ____________ of new facts. Students may also need to move away from earlier 33 ____________. In the physics example, some teachers ask students to identify the 34 ____________ in their first explanation. This makes the old idea available for 35 ____________. In this context, forgetting is not evidence of 36 ____________.

A addition
B misconceptions
C weak memory
D revision
E contradictions
F punishment
G expertise
H technology
I confidence

Questions 37-40
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 3?
In boxes 37-40 on your answer sheet, write

TRUE if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this

37 The writer believes that all forms of forgetting are beneficial.
38 In the Carden study, the group asked to avoid old routes made fewer wrong turns by the end of the final task.
39 Participants in the Carden study were paid according to how accurately they completed the final task.
40 The writer suggests that keeping outdated knowledge can sometimes prevent people from adapting.
rawAnswerKey:
Question 27: The writer begins with the student at the kitchen table in order to show that

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: a parent says what many people have been told since childhood: "You just need to remember more." The advice sounds harmless, even obvious. Schools reward recall, workplaces value stored expertise

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ scene เปิดเรื่องให้ parent พูดว่า You just need to remember more และ Schools reward recall ซึ่ง paraphrase ว่าคนส่วนใหญ่เชื่อว่าการเรียนรู้ขึ้นกับการจำมากขึ้น ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ passage ไม่ได้บอกว่าวิธีเรียนของนักเรียนไม่มีประสิทธิภาพ ตัวเลือก C ไม่ได้พูดว่าพ่อแม่เข้าใจวิชาผิด ตัวเลือก D ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบว่าวิชาประวัติศาสตร์จำยากกว่าวิชาอื่น

Paraphrased Vocabulary: assume learning depends on remembering more = You just need to remember more / Schools reward recall; kitchen table scene = opening student example; answer signal = B



Question 28: What point does the writer make in the second paragraph?

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: these examples can lead to a misleading conclusion: that the best possible mind would be one that kept everything. A mind unable to let go of irrelevant material would soon become crowded with details that no longer help it act. The point is not that forgetting is always good, but that memory without selection would be almost unusable.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะย่อหน้าสองชี้ว่า mind ที่เก็บทุกอย่างจะ crowded และ memory without selection would be almost unusable ซึ่งตรง remembering everything would not necessarily be useful ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ passage บอก forgetting มี reputation แย่แต่ไม่ได้สรุปว่า harmful มากกว่าที่คิด ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะไม่ได้สนับสนุนการเก็บรายละเอียดเล็ก ๆ ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะแยก ordinary forgetting จาก carelessness ในย่อหน้าถัดไป ไม่ใช่ย่อหน้านี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: remembering everything = kept everything; not necessarily useful = almost unusable / crowded with irrelevant details; answer signal = C



Question 29: What does Dr Lena Roth's phrase "cognitive housekeeping" suggest?

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: Dr Lena Roth... describes useful forgetting as a kind of "cognitive housekeeping". By this she does not mean that the mind throws away information at random. Rather, it weakens access to material that is outdated, distracting, or no longer connected to present goals.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะ Roth อธิบาย cognitive housekeeping ว่า mind weakens access to outdated or distracting material ไม่ใช่ทิ้งข้อมูลแบบสุ่ม ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะไม่ได้พูดถึงลำดับความจำแบบคงที่ ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะไม่ได้เชื่อมกับความเหนื่อยล้าทางกาย ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะ passage แยก ordinary forgetting จาก mental decline ชัดเจน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: reduce access to no longer useful information = weakens access to outdated material; cognitive housekeeping = sorting not random throwing away; answer signal = A



Question 30: What did the Carden Research Centre study suggest?

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: In the final navigation task, this second group was slower at the beginning, but by the end they made fewer wrong turns and adapted better to the changed layout. Innes did not claim that forgetting makes people more intelligent. His conclusion was more cautious: when circumstances change, deliberately weakening old responses may make new learning more flexible.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ Innes สรุปอย่างระมัดระวังว่า deliberately weakening old responses may make new learning more flexible ซึ่งตรง outdated responses deliberately weakened ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ passage ระบุชัดว่า Innes did not claim that forgetting makes people more intelligent และไม่ได้ใช้คำว่า always ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะไม่ได้เปรียบ digital กับ real-world navigation ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะทั้งสองกลุ่มถูกบอกว่า museum renovated

Paraphrased Vocabulary: outdated responses weakened = describe old routes and deliberately avoid using them; new learning more flexible = adapted better to the changed layout; answer signal = B



Question 31: What is the writer's main point about experienced technicians?

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: Their difficulty is not lack of knowledge but the persistence of knowledge that once worked well.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ Mendez พบว่า technicians ลำบากเพราะ persistence of knowledge that once worked well ไม่ใช่ lack of knowledge ซึ่งตรง previous knowledge may interfere with adaptation ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะไม่ได้บอกว่า resist training เพราะ dislike technology ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะ passage บอก previous expertise still has value ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะปัญหาคือ old habits ไม่ใช่ poor safety instructions

Paraphrased Vocabulary: previous knowledge interferes = persistence of knowledge that once worked well; adaptation = replaced machine / warning sound no longer produced; answer signal = B



Question 32: learning should not be seen only as the .................... of new facts.

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: Learning is often described as the addition of new facts

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A (addition) เพราะ summary ถาม ___ of new facts ซึ่งตรง Learning is often described as the addition of new facts ใน word bank ต้องเลือก A ไม่ใช่ G expertise หรือ I confidence ที่ไม่มีในประโยคนี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: ___ of new facts = addition of new facts; word bank trap = G expertise / I confidence; answer signal = A



Question 33: Students may also need to move away from earlier .................... .

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: progress may also require students to loosen their grip on earlier misconceptions.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B (misconceptions) เพราะ students ต้อง loosen their grip on earlier misconceptions ตรง move away from earlier ___ ตัวเลือก F punishment ผิดเพราะ passage บอก aim is not to shame them

Paraphrased Vocabulary: move away from earlier ___ = loosen their grip on earlier misconceptions; word bank = B misconceptions



Question 34: teachers ask students to identify the .................... in their first explanation.

Correct Answer: E

Accepted Answers: E

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: first ask students to mark the contradictions in their original explanation.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ E (contradictions) เพราะ teachers ask students to mark the contradictions in their original explanation ตรง identify the ___ ไม่ใช่ D revision ที่เป็นผลลัพธ์ของขั้นตอนนี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: identify the ___ = mark the contradictions; revision = later step (blank 35); answer signal = E



Question 35: This makes the old idea available for .................... .

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: make the old idea available for revision.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D (revision) เพราะ old idea available for revision หลังระบุ contradictions ไม่ใช่ E contradictions ที่ใช้ไปแล้วในข้อ 34

Paraphrased Vocabulary: available for ___ = available for revision; word bank = D revision



Question 36: forgetting is not evidence of .................... .

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: In such cases, forgetting is not a sign of weak memory.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C (weak memory) เพราะ passage ระบุ forgetting is not a sign of weak memory ตรง not evidence of ___ ไม่ใช่ F punishment หรือ B misconceptions

Paraphrased Vocabulary: not evidence of ___ = not a sign of weak memory; word bank = C weak memory



Question 37: The writer believes that all forms of forgetting are beneficial.

Correct Answer: FALSE

Accepted Answers: FALSE

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: The point is not that forgetting is always good... None of this should romanticise memory loss. Forgetting can be painful, especially when it affects identity, relationships, or health.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ FALSE เพราะ passage บอกชัดว่า The point is not that forgetting is always good และไม่ควร romanticise memory loss ขัดกับ all forms of forgetting are beneficial ถ้าเลือก TRUE จะข้ามประโยคปฏิเสธนี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: all forms beneficial = forgetting is always good; contradicts = forgetting can be painful / should not romanticise memory loss



Question 38: In the Carden study, the group asked to avoid old routes made fewer wrong turns by the end of the final task.

Correct Answer: TRUE

Accepted Answers: TRUE

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: this second group was slower at the beginning, but by the end they made fewer wrong turns and adapted better to the changed layout.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ TRUE เพราะ second group (avoid old routes) by the end made fewer wrong turns แม้ช้ากว่าตอนต้นก็ตาม ตัวเลือก FALSE ผิดถ้าเอา slower at the beginning มาตีความว่าทำได้แย่กว่าโดยรวม

Paraphrased Vocabulary: avoid old routes = describe old routes and deliberately avoid using them; fewer wrong turns by the end = made fewer wrong turns by the end



Question 39: Participants in the Carden study were paid according to how accurately they completed the final task.

Correct Answer: NOT GIVEN

Accepted Answers: NOT GIVEN

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: Seventy-two volunteers first learned the layout of a virtual museum, including the locations of several rooms and staircases.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ NOT GIVEN เพราะ passage อธิบาย seventy-two volunteers และผล navigation แต่ไม่กล่าวเรื่อง payment ตาม accuracy คำว่า paid/accuracy ไม่ปรากฏในบทความ แม้มี volunteers ก็ไม่ตอบข้อนี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: paid according to accuracy = not mentioned; NOT GIVEN = volunteers described but no payment



Question 40: The writer suggests that keeping outdated knowledge can sometimes prevent people from adapting.

Correct Answer: TRUE

Accepted Answers: TRUE

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - The Quiet Skill of Forgetting

Exact Portion: Their difficulty is not lack of knowledge but the persistence of knowledge that once worked well... people may need not only to acquire knowledge, but also to release knowledge that has stopped serving them.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ TRUE เพราะ writer สรุปว่าต้อง release knowledge that has stopped serving them และตัวอย่าง technicians แสดง old knowledge ขัดกับการปรับตัว ตรง keeping outdated knowledge can prevent adapting

Paraphrased Vocabulary: keeping outdated knowledge = persistence of knowledge that once worked well; prevent adapting = struggle when machine replaced / release knowledge
